EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm activity on October 13th produced several funnel clouds as well as damaging wind gusts and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked intermittently for nearly two miles from just north of HWY 13 through the buns slough area of central Denver Township, o around 5 NNE of Crete by 408 PM CDT. The tornado repeatedly showed a dust plume but stayed mainly over open grassland. Peak winds estimated to 70 mph, with a max width of 25 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm activity on October 13th produced several funnel clouds as well as damaging wind gusts and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One or more developing funnels were noted by trained spotters to the southwest and west of Wahpeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm activity on October 13th produced several funnel clouds as well as damaging wind gusts and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent funnel cloud was noted tracking over open country northwest of Wahpeton and northeast of Dwight. One or more brief non-damaging touchdowns may have occurred. Reports and photos were posted via social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained storm spotter reported an estimated 70 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust was reported by a personal weather station in Hankinson.

Repeated shots of bitterly cold arctic air penetrated through northeast and east-central ND. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 15F to minus 30F with wind chills ranging from minus 40F to minus 55F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 35F to minus 48F, each day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid arctic high pressure situated along the lee of the Canadian Rockies into northeast Montana gradually pushed over the Northern Plains, bringing life threatening cold to western and central North Dakota. This after what had been a mild winter to that point. Initially, the coldest wind chills were found primarily over the northern portions of the area, but reinforcing rounds of cold air continued to push through, resulting in the life threatening cold pushing into the southern part of the state. Wind chill values to 60 below were experienced over northern locations, with values to 50 below over southern locations. Minot experienced twelve days in a row with low temperatures of 10 below zero or lower, with Bismarck experiencing ten days in a row of these conditions. These long runs of cold temperatures were attained with little to no snow on the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dipped to around 50 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of dense fog combined with below freezing temperatures led to rime ice forming on power lines over portions of south central North Dakota into the southern James River Valley. Many power lines had rime ice accumulations of over three inches  diameter, which led to lines sagging and some snapping. This included not only distribution lines, but also main transmission lines which feed power to substations. Approximately 2,000 electric customers suffered power outages, with some outages lasting a couple of days. Through the event, linemen were repairing snapped lines and manually clearing ice off the lines to prevent further breakage. In addition, 14 wooden power poles were broken from the weight of the ice covered lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of freezing fog led to rime ice accumulation on power lines in LaMoure and Dickey counties. Approximately 1,300 services in total between the two counties experienced outages. Six power poles, in total, were broken, and some substations went down due to transmission line outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest arctic air of the season settled across eastern ND. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 25F to minus 48F with wind chills ranging from minus 45F to minus 60F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 40F to minus 55F, each day. Air temperatures of minus 40F or colder were recorded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 35F to minus 50F, each day. Air temperatures of minus 35F or colder were recorded.

Initially, the coldest wind chills were found primarily over the northern portions of the area, but reinforcing rounds of cold air continued to push through, resulting in the life threatening cold pushing into the southern part of the state. Wind chill values to 60 below were experienced over northern locations, with values to 50 below over southern locations. Minot experienced twelve days in a row with low temperatures of 10 below zero or lower, with Bismarck experiencing ten days in a row of these conditions. These long runs of cold temperatures were attained with little to no snow on the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dipped to around 50 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of dense fog combined with below freezing temperatures led to rime ice forming on power lines over portions of south central North Dakota into the southern James River Valley. Many power lines had rime ice accumulations of over three inches  diameter, which led to lines sagging and some snapping. This included not only distribution lines, but also main transmission lines which feed power to substations. Approximately 2,000 electric customers suffered power outages, with some outages lasting a couple of days. Through the event, linemen were repairing snapped lines and manually clearing ice off the lines to prevent further breakage. In addition, 14 wooden power poles were broken from the weight of the ice covered lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of freezing fog led to rime ice accumulation on power lines. This led to 707 services and three substations loosing power in the McIntosh, Logan, and Emmons County areas. McIntosh County was hardest hit with the loss of eight power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient formed over the region as a surface low gradually passed to the north. A cold front pushing through the area resulted in some light showers developing which enhanced the mixing of stronger winds aloft to the surface. This led to some strong wind gusts developing. The strongest wind gust was 69 mph which occurred in Adams County at the Hettinger Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hettinger ASOS reported wind gusts to 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Repeated shots of bitterly cold arctic air penetrated through northeast and east-central ND. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 15F to minus 30F with wind chills ranging from minus 40F to minus 55F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 40F to minus 55F, each day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough and associated cold front pushed across the CWA on the morning of June 11th. Severe thunderstorms developed along the pre-frontal trough, as well as the cold front. Numerous wind damage, and hail reports were submitted, as well as a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large, empty grain bins at an elevator complex were caved in and collapsed. Peak winds estimated to be 80 mph, with multiple long duration gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season Winter Storm moved into the Northern Plains on the evening of the 11th, producing widespread rain across portions of eastern ND and northwest through west-central MN, along with strong gusty winds, and areas of light snow. In addition, the widespread strong and gusty winds produced a few severe wind gusts at 58 mph or greater at select locations, mainly during the overnight period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind measured at the Grand Forks International Airport /KGFK/. Wind measured during an ongoing winter weather advisory with light snow occurring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter season storm brought strong winds to much of eastern ND and northwest through west-central MN, through the evening of the 16th through the morning of the 17th.  A few locations in the Devils Lake Basin of northeast ND experienced high peak winds speeds early in the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind measured by the NDDOT RWIS station located south of Devils Lake, near Camp Grafton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moved along the Canadian border as a strong cold front swept through North Dakota. This led to strong winds developing over western and central North Dakota including the James River Valley. Additionally, the mixing of stronger winds aloft down to the surface was occasionally aided by light rain and snow showers moving through, enhancing wind gusts. The strongest reported wind gust of 72 mph occurred in Divide County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bottineau County had estimated gusts to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through western and central North Dakota during the evening and nighttime hours of January 13 into the early morning of January 14. Damaging winds developed in the wake of the cold front as cold air pushed in. Some rain and snow showers also developed, which aided in strong winds aloft mixing down to the surface, especially over northwest North Dakota. Multiple occurrences of damage from the strong winds were reported. Many places had 80 mph wind gusts with the highest being 93 mph at the Williston Airport in Williams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crosby AWOS reported wind gusts to 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest arctic air of the season settled across eastern ND. Minimum morning temperatures ranged from minus 25F to minus 48F with wind chills ranging from minus 45F to minus 60F.  Daytime high temperatures remained well below zero in most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observer, AWOS, and Mesonet sensors reported morning low wind chill temperatures of from minus 40F to minus 55F, each day. Air temperatures of minus 40F or colder were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in the vicinity of a warm front lifting through southern North Dakota as a short-wave trough approached the area. Strong instability and deep layer wind shear were noted. The highest wind gust was estimated to be 75 mph, which occurred in far southwest Morton County southwest of Flasher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in the vicinity of a warm front lifting through southern North Dakota as a short-wave trough approached the area. Strong instability and deep layer wind shear were noted. The highest wind gust was estimated to be 75 mph, which occurred in far southwest Morton County southwest of Flasher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree on the end of a shelterbelt broke off just above ground level. A wind break panel was torn off and blown over a fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along and ahead of a slow moving frontal boundary draped through the upper James River Valley into south central North Dakota. Along the boundary, strong low level instability and surface vorticity were noted. One thunderstorm produced a tornado southeast of Tappen in Kidder County. The tornado moved slowly through an open field, stretching westerly as it dissipated. No damage was reported. The tornado was highly visible, and lasted about nine minutes. It was rated EFU, EF Unknown, by National Weather Service policy as there was no damage to evaluate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down in an open field southeast of Tappen. There was no damage to evaluate and so by National Weather Service policy the tornado is rated EF Unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May started with a D3 drought over all of western and central North Dakota. Of the 36 NWS Bismarck forecast area counties, 34 were entirely in D3 with only the eastern parts of LaMoure and Dickey counties still in D2. By the middle of the month D4 drought designation was introduced into parts of the area between Bismarck and the Canadian border. By the end of May rains in the Dickinson area and parts of the James River Valley caused those areas to be reverted back to D2, at least parts of some counties. D4 continued between Bismarck and the Canadian border.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell into the upper 20s to low 30s on the morning of May 27th.  The NDAWN station 1 W of Wales measured a low of 29F and the NDAWN station 1 E of Langdon measured 31F, while personal weather station across northern portions of the county measured as low at 29F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May started with a D3 drought over all of western and central North Dakota. Of the 36 NWS Bismarck forecast area counties, 34 were entirely in D3 with only the eastern parts of LaMoure and Dickey counties still in D2. By the middle of the month D4 drought designation was introduced into parts of the area between Bismarck and the Canadian border. By the end of May rains in the Dickinson area and parts of the James River Valley caused those areas to be reverted back to D2, at least parts of some counties. D4 continued between Bismarck and the Canadian border.

An extended period consisting of two days of continuous severe weather occurred from June 7 through June 8. Thunderstorms initially developed along a warm front over the James River Valley into parts of south central North Dakota where strong instability and deep layer shear were noted. During the evening, a mix of wind and hail damage occurred. The largest hail reported during this period was 2.25 inches in diameter which occurred in Logan County, while the strongest reported wind gust occurred in LaMoure County, which was estimated at 80 mph. In addition a tornado occurred in Stutsman County. The tornado caused no damage and was rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the night went on, a strong low level jet sustained severe thunderstorms. These continued into the morning, before storms started gradually spreading to the west along the slow moving, if not stationary, warm front that initiated the storms the previous evening. From early morning into the afternoon, large hail and damaging wind gusts remained a concern, but the primary threat became flooding due to the slow moving storms gradually back-building towards the west. The initial flooding occurred over the southern James River Valley, particularly near the city of Marion in LaMoure County. The flooding concerns then gradually spread west into Kidder, Emmons, Burleigh, and Grant counties. Later in the afternoon into the evening and nighttime hours as the storms developed over far western North Dakota, the main threats changed back to large hail and damaging wind gusts. During this time period, the largest reported hail was the size of a baseball, which fell in Golden Valley County. The strongest reported wind gust was estimated to be at 80 mph, in Billings County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A majority of the second story of an older barn was destroyed. A tree was down and a 500 gallon propane tank was moved on top of the tree. A corral was damaged, and some sheet metal blew off of a machine shed. A power pole with transformer was also toppled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short-wave trough moved into the Northern Plains as a slow moving warm front lifted through North Dakota. Very strong instability developed by afternoon, along with increased deep layer shear. The initial storms occurred over west-central and northwest North Dakota. The main concern with these storms was large hail and tornadoes. Multiple tornadoes occurred over western McKenzie County over a similar location, and one occurred in Williams County. Photos of the tornadoes submitted to the National Weather Service indicate that one of them in McKenzie County was quite large. With that said, no damage was reported given the tornadoes occurred in rugged terrain over a rural area, so all were rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the evening went on, the threats shifted to a mix of strong winds, hail, and heavy rain. The city of Williston was hit the hardest with multiple storms passing over in a short period of time. Flash flooding occurred in the city, which floated vehicles and flooded basements. Hail to the size of tennis balls also fell, causing extensive home and vehicle damage. In addition, very strong winds were reported at the Williston airport, with gusts to 82 mph with one storm, then gusts to 93 mph with the next, causing roof damage. Later in the night the storms became more linear and grew towards the south, while the overall lines moved east. Wind became the primary concern as the storms moved towards the James River Valley where multiple wind damage events occurred. For the whole event, the largest hail reported was the size of tennis balls, in Williston in Williams County. The strongest reported wind gust was 93 mph at the Williston Airport, also in Williams County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short-wave trough passing through the area led to some very early morning thunderstorms with gusty winds developing. One storm produced severe wind gusts of 58 mph near Watford City in McKenzie County.

Severe thunderstorms occurred along a cold front that pushed into western and central North Dakota where strong instability and enhanced deep layer shear were noted. Strong wind gusts became the main threat, though one storm did produce large hail in Stark County. The strongest reported wind gusts, estimated at 70 mph, occurred at Beulah in Mercer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short-wave trough moved into the Northern Plains as a slow moving warm front lifted through North Dakota. Very strong instability developed by afternoon, along with increased deep layer shear. The initial storms occurred over west-central and northwest North Dakota. The main concern with these storms was large hail and tornadoes. Multiple tornadoes occurred over western McKenzie County over a similar location, and one occurred in Williams County. Photos of the tornadoes submitted to the National Weather Service indicate that one of them in McKenzie County was quite large. With that said, no damage was reported given the tornadoes occurred in rugged terrain over a rural area, so all were rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the evening went on, the threats shifted to a mix of strong winds, hail, and heavy rain. The city of Williston was hit the hardest with multiple storms passing over in a short period of time. Flash flooding occurred in the city, which floated vehicles and flooded basements. Hail to the size of tennis balls also fell, causing extensive home and vehicle damage. In addition, very strong winds were reported at the Williston airport, with gusts to 82 mph with one storm, then gusts to 93 mph with the next, causing roof damage. Later in the night the storms became more linear and grew towards the south, while the overall lines moved east. Wind became the primary concern as the storms moved towards the James River Valley where multiple wind damage events occurred. For the whole event, the largest hail reported was the size of tennis balls, in Williston in Williams County. The strongest reported wind gust was 93 mph at the Williston Airport, also in Williams County.

Thunderstorms initially developed along a warm front over the James River Valley into parts of south central North Dakota where strong instability and deep layer shear were noted. During the evening, a mix of wind and hail damage occurred. The largest hail reported during this period was 2.25 inches in diameter which occurred in Logan County, while the strongest reported wind gust occurred in LaMoure County, which was estimated at 80 mph. In addition a tornado occurred in Stutsman County. The tornado caused no damage and was rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the night went on, a strong low level jet sustained severe thunderstorms. These continued into the morning, before storms started gradually spreading to the west along the slow moving, if not stationary, warm front that initiated the storms the previous evening. From early morning into the afternoon, large hail and damaging wind gusts remained a concern, but the primary threat became flooding due to the slow moving storms gradually back-building towards the west. The initial flooding occurred over the southern James River Valley, particularly near the city of Marion in LaMoure County. The flooding concerns then gradually spread west into Kidder, Emmons, Burleigh, and Grant counties. Later in the afternoon into the evening and nighttime hours as the storms developed over far western North Dakota, the main threats changed back to large hail and damaging wind gusts. During this time period, the largest reported hail was the size of a baseball, which fell in Golden Valley County. The strongest reported wind gust was estimated to be at 80 mph, in Billings County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cellular phone tower was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short-wave trough moved into the Northern Plains as a slow moving warm front lifted through North Dakota. Very strong instability developed by afternoon, along with increased deep layer shear. The initial storms occurred over west-central and northwest North Dakota. The main concern with these storms was large hail and tornadoes. Multiple tornadoes occurred over western McKenzie County over a similar location, and one occurred in Williams County. Photos of the tornadoes submitted to the National Weather Service indicate that one of them in McKenzie County was quite large. With that said, no damage was reported given the tornadoes occurred in rugged terrain over a rural area, so all were rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the evening went on, the threats shifted to a mix of strong winds, hail, and heavy rain. The city of Williston was hit the hardest with multiple storms passing over in a short period of time. Flash flooding occurred in the city, which floated vehicles and flooded basements. Hail to the size of tennis balls also fell, causing extensive home and vehicle damage. In addition, very strong winds were reported at the Williston airport, with gusts to 82 mph with one storm, then gusts to 93 mph with the next, causing roof damage. Later in the night the storms became more linear and grew towards the south, while the overall lines moved east. Wind became the primary concern as the storms moved towards the James River Valley where multiple wind damage events occurred. For the whole event, the largest hail reported was the size of tennis balls, in Williston in Williams County. The strongest reported wind gust was 93 mph at the Williston Airport, also in Williams County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the first of multiple rounds of hail to strike the Williston area the evening of June 10. With this round, hail up to the size of tennis balls fell over the Williston area. This caused damage to many roofs and vehicles, including damaging every police car in the city, and every vehicle parked in the Williams County facility lot. In addition, strong winds with this storm blew a roof off a truck and car wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm clusters formed during the evening hours of June 5th, with several storms producing damaging wind gusts. This episode went through the early morning hours on June 6th. Hail up to one inch was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind was measured at a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period consisting of two days of continuous severe weather occurred from June 7 through June 8. Thunderstorms initially developed along a warm front over the James River Valley into parts of south central North Dakota where strong instability and deep layer shear were noted. During the evening, a mix of wind and hail damage occurred. The largest hail reported during this period was 2.25 inches in diameter which occurred in Logan County, while the strongest reported wind gust occurred in LaMoure County, which was estimated at 80 mph. In addition a tornado occurred in Stutsman County. The tornado caused no damage and was rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the night went on, a strong low level jet sustained severe thunderstorms. These continued into the morning, before storms started gradually spreading to the west along the slow moving, if not stationary, warm front that initiated the storms the previous evening. From early morning into the afternoon, large hail and damaging wind gusts remained a concern, but the primary threat became flooding due to the slow moving storms gradually back-building towards the west. The initial flooding occurred over the southern James River Valley, particularly near the city of Marion in LaMoure County. The flooding concerns then gradually spread west into Kidder, Emmons, Burleigh, and Grant counties. Later in the afternoon into the evening and nighttime hours as the storms developed over far western North Dakota, the main threats changed back to large hail and damaging wind gusts. During this time period, the largest reported hail was the size of a baseball, which fell in Golden Valley County. The strongest reported wind gust was estimated to be at 80 mph, in Billings County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short-wave trough moved into the Northern Plains as a slow moving warm front lifted through North Dakota. Very strong instability developed by afternoon, along with increased deep layer shear. The initial storms occurred over west-central and northwest North Dakota. The main concern with these storms was large hail and tornadoes. Multiple tornadoes occurred over western McKenzie County over a similar location, and one occurred in Williams County. Photos of the tornadoes submitted to the National Weather Service indicate that one of them in McKenzie County was quite large. With that said, no damage was reported given the tornadoes occurred in rugged terrain over a rural area, so all were rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the evening went on, the threats shifted to a mix of strong winds, hail, and heavy rain. The city of Williston was hit the hardest with multiple storms passing over in a short period of time. Flash flooding occurred in the city, which floated vehicles and flooded basements. Hail to the size of tennis balls also fell, causing extensive home and vehicle damage. In addition, very strong winds were reported at the Williston airport, with gusts to 82 mph with one storm, then gusts to 93 mph with the next, causing roof damage. Later in the night the storms became more linear and grew towards the south, while the overall lines moved east. Wind became the primary concern as the storms moved towards the James River Valley where multiple wind damage events occurred. For the whole event, the largest hail reported was the size of tennis balls, in Williston in Williams County. The strongest reported wind gust was 93 mph at the Williston Airport, also in Williams County.

A cold front swept through the Northern Plains from late Monday, March 29th, into Tuesday morning, March 30th.  Strong winds developed from the south on Monday with gusts into the 40 to 45 mph range, and switched from the northwest and peaked with the overnight frontal passage at from 55 to near 70 mph across portions pf northeast and east-central ND.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind measured at the Cando Airport.

With that said, no damage was reported given the tornadoes occurred in rugged terrain over a rural area, so all were rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the evening went on, the threats shifted to a mix of strong winds, hail, and heavy rain. The city of Williston was hit the hardest with multiple storms passing over in a short period of time. Flash flooding occurred in the city, which floated vehicles and flooded basements. Hail to the size of tennis balls also fell, causing extensive home and vehicle damage. In addition, very strong winds were reported at the Williston airport, with gusts to 82 mph with one storm, then gusts to 93 mph with the next, causing roof damage. Later in the night the storms became more linear and grew towards the south, while the overall lines moved east. Wind became the primary concern as the storms moved towards the James River Valley where multiple wind damage events occurred. For the whole event, the largest hail reported was the size of tennis balls, in Williston in Williams County. The strongest reported wind gust was 93 mph at the Williston Airport, also in Williams County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period consisting of two days of continuous severe weather occurred from June 7 through June 8. Thunderstorms initially developed along a warm front over the James River Valley into parts of south central North Dakota where strong instability and deep layer shear were noted. During the evening, a mix of wind and hail damage occurred. The largest hail reported during this period was 2.25 inches in diameter which occurred in Logan County, while the strongest reported wind gust occurred in LaMoure County, which was estimated at 80 mph. In addition a tornado occurred in Stutsman County. The tornado caused no damage and was rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the night went on, a strong low level jet sustained severe thunderstorms. These continued into the morning, before storms started gradually spreading to the west along the slow moving, if not stationary, warm front that initiated the storms the previous evening. From early morning into the afternoon, large hail and damaging wind gusts remained a concern, but the primary threat became flooding due to the slow moving storms gradually back-building towards the west. The initial flooding occurred over the southern James River Valley, particularly near the city of Marion in LaMoure County. The flooding concerns then gradually spread west into Kidder, Emmons, Burleigh, and Grant counties. Later in the afternoon into the evening and nighttime hours as the storms developed over far western North Dakota, the main threats changed back to large hail and damaging wind gusts. During this time period, the largest reported hail was the size of a baseball, which fell in Golden Valley County. The strongest reported wind gust was estimated to be at 80 mph, in Billings County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over four inches of rain was measured over various parts of southwest Kidder County, with radar estimates of over six inches. In the city of Steele, many roads were under water. Over rural portions of the county affected by the heavy rain, multiple gravel road and culvert washouts occurred.

A winter storm system produced a band of mixed rain and snow or sleet, which transitioned to heavy snow from late afternoon on the 10th, through the evening and overnight hours into the early morning hours of the 11th.  Heaviest snow lay along a band from central South Dakota, into the MN/ND/SD triple point and west central MN, and through northcentral MN into the Minnesota Arrowhead. This heavy band included far southeastern ND.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of from 6 to 9 inches was reported across the county.

The largest hail reported during this period was 2.25 inches in diameter which occurred in Logan County, while the strongest reported wind gust occurred in LaMoure County, which was estimated at 80 mph. In addition a tornado occurred in Stutsman County. The tornado caused no damage and was rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the night went on, a strong low level jet sustained severe thunderstorms. These continued into the morning, before storms started gradually spreading to the west along the slow moving, if not stationary, warm front that initiated the storms the previous evening. From early morning into the afternoon, large hail and damaging wind gusts remained a concern, but the primary threat became flooding due to the slow moving storms gradually back-building towards the west. The initial flooding occurred over the southern James River Valley, particularly near the city of Marion in LaMoure County. The flooding concerns then gradually spread west into Kidder, Emmons, Burleigh, and Grant counties. Later in the afternoon into the evening and nighttime hours as the storms developed over far western North Dakota, the main threats changed back to large hail and damaging wind gusts. During this time period, the largest reported hail was the size of a baseball, which fell in Golden Valley County. The strongest reported wind gust was estimated to be at 80 mph, in Billings County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over five inches of rain was measured over parts of west-central into northeast Emmons County, with over seven inches indicated on radar, leading to flash flooding. In Hazelton, streets and yards flooded. Near Braddock, a mail delivery vehicle was swept away in flood waters, along with another vehicle. Multiple rural roads were heavily damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short-wave trough moved into the Northern Plains as a slow moving warm front lifted through North Dakota. Very strong instability developed by afternoon, along with increased deep layer shear. The initial storms occurred over west-central and northwest North Dakota. The main concern with these storms was large hail and tornadoes. Multiple tornadoes occurred over western McKenzie County over a similar location, and one occurred in Williams County. Photos of the tornadoes submitted to the National Weather Service indicate that one of them in McKenzie County was quite large. With that said, no damage was reported given the tornadoes occurred in rugged terrain over a rural area, so all were rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the evening went on, the threats shifted to a mix of strong winds, hail, and heavy rain. The city of Williston was hit the hardest with multiple storms passing over in a short period of time. Flash flooding occurred in the city, which floated vehicles and flooded basements. Hail to the size of tennis balls also fell, causing extensive home and vehicle damage. In addition, very strong winds were reported at the Williston airport, with gusts to 82 mph with one storm, then gusts to 93 mph with the next, causing roof damage. Later in the night the storms became more linear and grew towards the south, while the overall lines moved east. Wind became the primary concern as the storms moved towards the James River Valley where multiple wind damage events occurred. For the whole event, the largest hail reported was the size of tennis balls, in Williston in Williams County. The strongest reported wind gust was 93 mph at the Williston Airport, also in Williams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches of rain fell in the Williston area as multiple rounds of thunderstorms passed through. This led to widespread street flooding, with water affecting many homes. In addition, multiple vehicles were damaged by the flood waters. This includes a combination of parked vehicles and ones where the driver entered the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through the Northern Plains from late Monday, March 29th, into Tuesday morning, March 30th.  Strong winds developed from the south on Monday with gusts into the 40 to 45 mph range, and switched from the northwest and peaked with the overnight frontal passage at from 55 to near 70 mph across portions pf northeast and east-central ND.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind measured at the Leeds RWIS located along U.S. Highway 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed near Hazen in Mercer County, became severe, and moved to the south-southwest along a corridor of elevated instability before rapidly dissipating as it moved through Stark County. The storm produced a swath of large hail along its path. The largest hail reported was the size of a hen egg, which occurred south-southwest of Beulah in Mercer County.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours over north central North Dakota along a variety of surface boundaries moving through the area. This was in an environment of very strong instability and deep layer shear. A few storms became severe, with the most intense storms occurring over McHenry County where reports of baseball size hail and estimated wind gusts to 75 mph occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July started with a D3 to D4 drought over most of western and central North Dakota. The exceptions were parts of the James River Valley, a large portion of southwest North Dakota, and a small portion of Northwest North Dakota. Most of the exception areas were in a D2 drought. By the end of the month changes included some improvement on the western edge of the D4 designation over much of the Lake Sakakawea region, though the eastern edge did expand some into Wells County.  In addition, the D3 designation expanded southeast farther into Foster, Stutsman, Dickey, and a small portion of LaMoure County.

A few storms became severe, with the most intense storms occurring over McHenry County where reports of baseball size hail and estimated wind gusts to 75 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts to 75 mph combined with hail to the size of golf balls to cause significant damage. Window screens were shredded on a home and a camper, windows were broken and siding damaged. A nearby potato field was shredded, and a corn field was about 50 percent damaged. Cottonwood trees were blown over, and cut hay was blown away.

July started with a D3 to D4 drought over most of western and central North Dakota. The exceptions were parts of the James River Valley, a large portion of southwest North Dakota, and a small portion of Northwest North Dakota. Most of the exception areas were in a D2 drought. By the end of the month changes included some improvement on the western edge of the D4 designation over much of the Lake Sakakawea region, though the eastern edge did expand some into Wells County.  In addition, the D3 designation expanded southeast farther into Foster, Stutsman, Dickey, and a small portion of LaMoure County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact short-wave trough moved over central North Dakota resulting in thunderstorm development. Instability was elevated and deep layer shear was marginal. This was enough, though, for large hail to develop over parts of the James River Valley. The largest hail was the size of a ping pong ball, which occurred northeast of Napoleon in Logan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in an area of modest instability and enhanced deep layer shear over southwest North Dakota as a short-wave trough approached. One storm became severe over Adams County, producing a wind gust of 63 mph at the Hettinger Airport.

An upper level low along with a surface trough approaching western North Dakota led to thunderstorms developing during the late afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe in an environment of strong instability and deep layer shear. The largest hail was the size of golf balls, and occurred in Billings and Slope counties. The strongest wind gust was 70 mph, and occurred near Regent in Hettinger County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in the early morning over far north central North Dakota along the nose of a low-level jet in an unstable environment with elevated  shear. The thunderstorms dropped one-inch diameter hail at Minot in Ward County before moving to the south-southeast. As one storm moved through the Sterling area in Burleigh County, strong wind gusts from the storms toppled multiple semi-trailers with estimated wind gusts of 75 mph. The thunderstorm then weakened and dissipated.

The thunderstorms dropped one-inch diameter hail at Minot in Ward County before moving to the south-southeast. As one storm moved through the Sterling area in Burleigh County, strong wind gusts from the storms toppled multiple semi-trailers with estimated wind gusts of 75 mph. The thunderstorm then weakened and dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed near Hazen in Mercer County, became severe, and moved to the south-southwest along a corridor of elevated instability before rapidly dissipating as it moved through Stark County. The storm produced a swath of large hail along its path. The largest hail reported was the size of a hen egg, which occurred south-southwest of Beulah in Mercer County.

Severe thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and early evening, extending into the overnight period. Damaging wind gusts, large hail, and tornadoes were all reported through the course of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tracked from northeast Coolin Township into east-central Klingstrup Township. It snapped or uprooted several large cottonwood trees at farmsteads and shelterbelts along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and early evening, extending into the overnight period. Damaging wind gusts, large hail, and tornadoes were all reported through the course of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tracked from far northeast Sharon Township across far northwest Westfield Township. A tornado Debris Signature was evident on KMVX radar. The tornado completely dismantled and destroyed a barn at one farmstead. It also snapped or uprooted numerous spruce, ash, cottonwood, and willow trees at various locations along its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system passed just to the north of the Canadian border, which led to a pair of cold fronts passing through North Dakota. The tight pressure gradient combined with the cold air pouring in behind the fronts led to strong winds over western and central North Dakota. The strongest wind gust was 72 mph in Divide County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bottineau County had estimated wind gusts to 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crosby RAWS reported a wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of snow passed over the region, with the first one affiliated with a warm front lifting through the area. Some locations then had a brief pause in snowfall, until the second round of snow affiliated with a surface low shifting from Wyoming into the Dakotas moved in. The combination of both these systems resulted in heavy snow over central and portions of western North Dakota. The heaviest reported snowfall of 18 inches occurred at Jamestown in Stutsman County. During the early morning of the 27th, blizzard conditions then developed over the James River Valley as strong winds increased. Conditions improved by the late afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emmons County received an estimated ten inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Airport, mesonet, and spotter reports confirmed visibilities were frequently below 1/4 mile in snow and blowing snow, starting between 8 and 10 am and continuing through the afternoon. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph for much of this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system passed just to the north of the Canadian border, which led to a pair of cold fronts passing through North Dakota. The tight pressure gradient combined with the cold air pouring in behind the fronts led to strong winds over western and central North Dakota. The strongest wind gust was 72 mph in Divide County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hettinger ASOS reported a wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of snow passed over the region, with the first one affiliated with a warm front lifting through the area. Some locations then had a brief pause in snowfall, until the second round of snow affiliated with a surface low shifting from Wyoming into the Dakotas moved in. The combination of both these systems resulted in heavy snow over central and portions of western North Dakota. The heaviest reported snowfall of 18 inches occurred at Jamestown in Stutsman County. During the early morning of the 27th, blizzard conditions then developed over the James River Valley as strong winds increased. Conditions improved by the late afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional falling snow along with fresh snow on the ground combined with strong winds to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours over central North Dakota where southerly low level flow increased instability, and deep layer shear was elevated. Initially, a nearly stationary supercell developed over Oliver County. The storm produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, along with torrential rain that led to flash flooding and damaging gravel roads. The storm eventually started moving to the south-southeast, continuing to produce severe weather in its path. Substantial damage occurred in Morton County north and east of the city of Flasher where hail up to the size of baseballs and wind gusts estimated to 80 mph severely damaged homes and vehicles, and overturned a semi-truck. Additional storms developed in the area. One intense supercell thunderstorm moved through Hettinger and Adams counties producing three inch diameter hail that destroyed crops and greatly damaged buildings and vehicles.

A few thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours over central North Dakota where southerly low level flow increased instability, and deep layer shear was elevated. Initially, a nearly stationary supercell developed over Oliver County. The storm produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, along with torrential rain that led to flash flooding and damaging gravel roads. The storm eventually started moving to the south-southeast, continuing to produce severe weather in its path. Substantial damage occurred in Morton County north and east of the city of Flasher where hail up to the size of baseballs and wind gusts estimated to 80 mph severely damaged homes and vehicles, and overturned a semi-truck. Additional storms developed in the area. One intense supercell thunderstorm moved through Hettinger and Adams counties producing three inch diameter hail that destroyed crops and greatly damaged buildings and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long lived supercell thunderstorm produced a path of destruction near Haynes. At one farm, large hail punched many holes through the metal roofs on pole barns and also into a hot-tub cover. Five vehicles were substantially damaged. Windows were broken on pole barns, and an exhaust vent for a pole barn furnace was busted off. To the south-southeast the destruction continued, with very large hail continuing to fall. In addition, strong thunderstorm winds accompanied the hail, shredding a field of lush eight foot tall corn, which was considered some of the nicest in the county, down to knee high.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August continued with D3 to D4 drought over most of western and central North Dakota, for at least part of the month. Parts of counties had more significant rain and saw the drought severity lessen to D2. There were three counties where the entire county lessened in severity to D2, Divide, LaMoure and Dickey. Parts of some counties saw the drought severity lessen to D1. By the end of the month rains came to central North Dakota, resulting in the area of D4 drought in that area shrinking in coverage. Over far western North Dakota, some areas saw a worsening of drought conditions, with much of Golden Valley County being introduced into a D4 designation. Refer to the U.S. Drought Monitor for details.

Thunderstorms developed along a surface trough over north-central North Dakota into the James River Valley region. Some of these storms produced very large hail in an environment of elevated instability and strong deep layer shear. The largest hail was produced by an intense thunderstorm that moved through eastern Wells County, resulting in baseball size hail in the city of Fessenden, and three inch diameter hail to the east of Bowdon.

Parts of counties had more significant rain and saw the drought severity lessen to D2. There were three counties where the entire county lessened in severity to D2, Divide, LaMoure and Dickey. Refer to the U.S. Drought Monitor for details.

Thunderstorms moved through the southern James River Valley as an upper level short-wave trough and affiliated surface low moved over the region. While most of the severe weather remained over South Dakota, one thunderstorm produced severe wind gusts estimated at 65 mph over the Ellendale area in Dickey County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some limbs were blown off a few trees, and an old tree blew over and damaged a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into North Dakota from South Dakota ahead of an approaching short-wave trough and cold front. One storm produced wind gusts of 69 mph in Sioux County.

Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours over northwest North Dakota as an upper level short-wave trough and affiliated surface low moved through the region. One storm became severe, producing half-dollar size hail south of Minot in Ward County.

Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours over northwest North Dakota as an upper level short-wave trough and affiliated surface low moved through the region. One storm became severe, producing half-dollar size hail south of Minot in Ward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some canola was shelled out.  Soybean leaves were damaged and some pods were knocked off plants.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave-trough moving through the region resulted in thunderstorms developing mainly over western South Dakota, but a few storms developed over far southwest North Dakota in an area of elevated instability. One storm became severe, producing a 60 mph wind gust at the Hettinger Airport in Adams County.

A frontal boundary moving through the region led to thunderstorms developing in an area of marginal instability but strong deep layer shear. A few storms became severe. The largest reported hail was the size of a ping pong ball, which fell in Williams County near Alamo. The strongest reported thunderstorm wind gust was estimated at 60 mph, which occurred in Mountrail County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours over central North Dakota where southerly low level flow increased instability, and deep layer shear was elevated. Initially, a nearly stationary supercell developed over Oliver County. The storm produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, along with torrential rain that led to flash flooding and damaging gravel roads. The storm eventually started moving to the south-southeast, continuing to produce severe weather in its path. Substantial damage occurred in Morton County north and east of the city of Flasher where hail up to the size of baseballs and wind gusts estimated to 80 mph severely damaged homes and vehicles, and overturned a semi-truck. Additional storms developed in the area. One intense supercell thunderstorm moved through Hettinger and Adams counties producing three inch diameter hail that destroyed crops and greatly damaged buildings and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed during the late afternoon and early evening, extending into the overnight period. Damaging wind gusts, large hail, and tornadoes were all reported through the course of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain and strong winds occurred, along with hail up to the size of ping pong balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours over central North Dakota where southerly low level flow increased instability, and deep layer shear was elevated. Initially, a nearly stationary supercell developed over Oliver County. The storm produced large hail and damaging wind gusts, along with torrential rain that led to flash flooding and damaging gravel roads. The storm eventually started moving to the south-southeast, continuing to produce severe weather in its path. Substantial damage occurred in Morton County north and east of the city of Flasher where hail up to the size of baseballs and wind gusts estimated to 80 mph severely damaged homes and vehicles, and overturned a semi-truck. Additional storms developed in the area. One intense supercell thunderstorm moved through Hettinger and Adams counties producing three inch diameter hail that destroyed crops and greatly damaged buildings and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September started with most of central and part of western North Dakota in a D3 drought, with a small part of the central and far southwest in D4. The rest of the area was designated D2. Through the month some areas received significant rain, but not the northwest. By the end of the month the only D4 designation was in Golden Valley County. Much of the west and north was D3, with the south in D2. At least some part of every county was in D2 or worse drought. Refer to the U.S. Drought Monitor for details.

October started with a small area of D4 designation in Golden Valley County, much of western and north central North Dakota in D3, and remaining areas in D2. At least some part of every county was in D2 or worse drought to start the month. The first half of the month saw notable precipitation over much of the area, which led to improvement in drought designation for most locations. The exception would be the far northwest, which continued to miss the heavier precipitation. Multiple counties over the south improved to either D1, D0, or no drought. By the end of the month, the only D3 areas were far northwest North Dakota, and the portion of Golden Valley County that started the month in D4. D2 designated drought continued mainly along and north of Interstate 94, and over the far southwest. Refer to the U.S. Drought Monitor for details.

Severe thunderstorm activity produced damaging wind gusts and tornadoes on October 9th across portions of southeastern North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter submitted photos and a GIF of a brief tornado via social media. Winds estimated at 70 mph.

A deep low pressure system that gradually lifted northeast through eastern North Dakota led to precipitation developing over much of the state. Some of the precipitation fell as snow, with much melting due to mild temperatures and warm ground. Over far southwest North Dakota, near the Montana border, snowfall rates were high enough to overcome the melting, and heavy wet snow accumulated. The highest reported accumulation was eight inches, which fell in Golden Valley County, north of Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locations north of Beach received eight inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of snow passed over the region, with the first one affiliated with a warm front lifting through the area. Some locations then had a brief pause in snowfall, until the second round of snow affiliated with a surface low shifting from Wyoming into the Dakotas moved in. The combination of both these systems resulted in heavy snow over central and portions of western North Dakota. The heaviest reported snowfall of 18 inches occurred at Jamestown in Stutsman County. During the early morning of the 27th, blizzard conditions then developed over the James River Valley as strong winds increased. Conditions improved by the late afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bismarck received 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started with a D3 to D4 drought over almost all of western and central North Dakota. Of the 36 NWS Bismarck forecast area counties, 31 were almost entirely in D3 or D4, with the exceptions being parts of Stark, Hettinger, Stutsman, LaMoure and Dickey counties in D2. By the middle of the month, some rain had fallen over southwest and portions of northwest North Dakota. As a result, parts of southwest North Dakota and a part of the northwest improved to D2, through the rest of the month. The area with the D4 designation which stretched from the Lake Sakakawea region into much of north central North Dakota did not change much through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June started with a D3 to D4 drought over almost all of western and central North Dakota. Of the 36 NWS Bismarck forecast area counties, 31 were almost entirely in D3 or D4, with the exceptions being parts of Stark, Hettinger, Stutsman, LaMoure and Dickey counties in D2. By the middle of the month, some rain had fallen over southwest and portions of northwest North Dakota. As a result, parts of southwest North Dakota and a part of the northwest improved to D2, through the rest of the month. The area with the D4 designation which stretched from the Lake Sakakawea region into much of north central North Dakota did not change much through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gradual improvement occurred in the drought through the month of December, mainly over central North Dakota. Drought at the beginning of December was similar to the end of November. By the second and third weeks of the month improvement occurred over the central parts of the state, to D1 and D0. Golden Valley County that was in D3 improved to D2. Parts or all of Divide, Burke, Renville, Williams, Mountrail, and McKenzie counties remained in D3. Refer to the U.S. Drought Monitor for details.

A strong short-wave trough moved into the Northern Plains as a slow moving warm front lifted through North Dakota. Very strong instability developed by afternoon, along with increased deep layer shear. The initial storms occurred over west-central and northwest North Dakota. The main concern with these storms was large hail and tornadoes. Multiple tornadoes occurred over western McKenzie County over a similar location, and one occurred in Williams County. Photos of the tornadoes submitted to the National Weather Service indicate that one of them in McKenzie County was quite large. With that said, no damage was reported given the tornadoes occurred in rugged terrain over a rural area, so all were rated EF-U, EF Unknown. As the evening went on, the threats shifted to a mix of strong winds, hail, and heavy rain. The city of Williston was hit the hardest with multiple storms passing over in a short period of time. Flash flooding occurred in the city, which floated vehicles and flooded basements. Hail to the size of tennis balls also fell, causing extensive home and vehicle damage. In addition, very strong winds were reported at the Williston airport, with gusts to 82 mph with one storm, then gusts to 93 mph with the next, causing roof damage. Later in the night the storms became more linear and grew towards the south, while the overall lines moved east. Wind became the primary concern as the storms moved towards the James River Valley where multiple wind damage events occurred. For the whole event, the largest hail reported was the size of tennis balls, in Williston in Williams County. The strongest reported wind gust was 93 mph at the Williston Airport, also in Williams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down and moved through open rangeland in rugged terrain southwest of Sather Dam, near the location of the previous tornado report. This tornado was noted to be quite large, and long lasting. There was no damage to evaluate and so by National Weather Service policy the tornado is rated EF Unknown.

A persistent band of snow moved north from South Dakota into southeast and far south central North Dakota, with Dickey County on the western end of the heaviest snowfall.  The band then stalled, and remained over Dickey County through the day before eventually departing to the east and dissipating. Around 13 inches of snow fell in the Ellendale area, with 11 inches in Oakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 13 inches of snow fell in the Ellendale area, with 11 inches reported at Oakes.
